claim: Protein language models trained with masked language modeling on evolutionary sequence diversity learn biological structure and function without explicit supervision
actionable: Apply masked language modeling objectives to domain sequence data (proteins, DNA, RNA) to discover learned representations of function and structure
source_title: 🔬 The Bitter Lesson is Coming for Proteins - Alex Rives, BioHub

claim: Scaling laws for protein models hold across parameter orders of magnitude, with emergent new capabilities appearing at 10x scale increases
actionable: Invest in training larger protein models; emergence of structure prediction and function inference happens predictably with scale
source_title: 🔬 The Bitter Lesson is Coming for Proteins - Alex Rives, BioHub

claim: Metagenomics-sourced training data (from extreme environments: hot vents, polar regions, soil, ocean) contains more diverse protein sequences than curated databases like UniProt
actionable: Include metagenomic sequences in training datasets for maximum evolutionary diversity; prioritize noisy environmental samples over clean database sequences
source_title: 🔬 The Bitter Lesson is Coming for Proteins - Alex Rives, BioHub

claim: ESM2 showed diminishing returns with scale on UniProt-only data; ESMC eliminated diminishing returns by adding metagenomic sequences, proving data quality matters more than compute in this regime
actionable: If scaling stalls on a curated dataset, source new data types rather than increasing compute; this often breaks through plateaus
source_title: 🔬 The Bitter Lesson is Coming for Proteins - Alex Rives, BioHub

claim: Sparse autoencoders on protein language models reveal a hierarchical feature space that mirrors biological knowledge without explicit supervision
actionable: Apply mechanistic interpretability (SAEs) to protein models to discover and validate emergent biological features; use this to build physics-informed abstractions
source_title: 🔬 The Bitter Lesson is Coming for Proteins - Alex Rives, BioHub

claim: Models discover single unified features for functional motifs (e.g., nucleophilic elbow) that appear across evolutionarily distant proteins, enabling functional transfer learning
actionable: Use learned feature representations to find functional homologies across proteins regardless of sequence similarity; apply this for protein engineering
source_title: 🔬 The Bitter Lesson is Coming for Proteins - Alex Rives, BioHub

claim: Distributional semantics theory (Zellig Harris, 1954) applies to proteins: amino acid context sets are determined by structure and function, so statistical patterns in sequences mirror underlying biology
actionable: Frame protein sequence prediction as learning from distributional constraints; this explains why language models work for biology
source_title: 🔬 The Bitter Lesson is Coming for Proteins - Alex Rives, BioHub

claim: ESMFold 2 achieves competitive structure prediction without multiple sequence alignments (MSAs), whereas AlphaFold requires them; this enables design of antibodies where MSAs fail
actionable: For protein design tasks where MSAs are unavailable (antibodies, de novo proteins), use transformer-only models without MSA requirements instead of AlphaFold-style approaches
source_title: 🔬 The Bitter Lesson is Coming for Proteins - Alex Rives, BioHub

claim: ESM-based world modeling can design functional antibodies (SCFVs) with therapeutic-level affinity by searching learned protein space for designs meeting binding criteria
actionable: For protein design, train generative models as world models and search them inversely; don't use forward simulation alone
source_title: 🔬 The Bitter Lesson is Coming for Proteins - Alex Rives, BioHub

claim: ESMC atlas covers 6.8 billion proteins clustered at 70% sequence identity (1.1 billion unique clusters), representing comprehensive evolutionary diversity
actionable: When building protein datasets, use 70% sequence identity clustering to capture diversity while avoiding redundancy; 1.1B clusters is near-complete coverage
source_title: 🔬 The Bitter Lesson is Coming for Proteins - Alex Rives, BioHub

claim: Earth's microbial diversity (viruses, bacteria, extreme-environment organisms) generates enormous protein sequence diversity that has barely been sampled; scaling is not bottlenecked by data availability
actionable: Plan for 10-100x more protein sequence data; focus on improving sampling from extreme environments and microbiomes rather than exhausting current databases
source_title: 🔬 The Bitter Lesson is Coming for Proteins - Alex Rives, BioHub

claim: Protein design models benefit from small sequence variations more than large architectural changes; single mutations are sufficient to destroy function, making fine-grained variation critical
actionable: When training protein models, don't oversample redundant sequences; preserve variation at single-mutation granularity for function prediction
source_title: 🔬 The Bitter Lesson is Coming for Proteins - Alex Rives, BioHub

claim: Virtual cell models trained on current data can represent but not generalize beyond training contexts; they lack predictive power for novel interventions needed for scientific discovery
actionable: Before deploying cellular models, verify they can predict outcomes of unseen interventions in unseen contexts; validation on training data distribution is insufficient
source_title: 🔬 The Bitter Lesson is Coming for Proteins - Alex Rives, BioHub

claim: Bridging molecular-scale protein interactions to cellular-scale behavior requires modeling protein-protein dynamics, not just static structures; information-theoretic models may be more tractable than physics-first simulation
actionable: For cellular modeling, prioritize information-theoretic approaches (genomic programs, signaling cascades) over first-principles physics simulation; build in layers from molecular to systems
source_title: 🔬 The Bitter Lesson is Coming for Proteins - Alex Rives, BioHub

claim: BioHub's Virtual Biology Initiative ($400M internal + $100M external) focuses on speed of data generation, generalization to novel interventions, spatial biology, and multi-modal measurement
actionable: For biology AI, invest in measurement technology and perturbation biology infrastructure; compute and models are secondary to data generation speed
source_title: 🔬 The Bitter Lesson is Coming for Proteins - Alex Rives, BioHub

claim: ESM models are fully open-source under MIT license, enabling community adoption and extending protein science to non-specialists with domain knowledge but limited ML expertise
actionable: Use open ESM models for rapid prototyping of protein design applications; democratize access beyond ML specialists
source_title: 🔬 The Bitter Lesson is Coming for Proteins - Alex Rives, BioHub
